data.title: Wall Street's major averages slide after briefly tagging record highs as payrolls data lands
data.description:
Wall Street’s broader market averages pare back earlier gains on Friday after all three major market averages breifly notched record highs all while yields slump as the jobs report came in cooler than expected.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) trades lower by 0.5%. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is in the red by 0.5%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) has slid 0.3%.

From a sector-by-sector perspective, five of the 11 S&P segments are trading in positive territory, led by Real Estate. At the other end of the spectrum, Energy has lagged the most so far.

In the bond market, yields have dropped as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped 9 basis points to 4.06%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down 11 basis points to 3.47%. Both the US2Y and US10Y have tagged 5-month lows.

On the economic front, August’s nonfarm payrolls report showed the economy added only 22,000 jobs—well below the 75,000 consensus forecast and trailing July’s 79,000 increase. At the same time, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.3% from 4.2% in July, matching expectations.

"Friday's jobs report showed that employers are still hesitant to hire and is further confirmation that the labor market is weakening. This is the last jobs report before the Federal Reserve's September meeting and its weakness increases the chances that the Fed will proceed with its rate cut later this month," Rich Mullen, CEO, Pallas Capital Advisors stated.

Traders are now fully pricing in a rate cut in September after the latest labor report came in cooler than expected.

As for stocks that are on the move, Broadcom (AVGO) is up 9.1% after fiscal third quarter results beat estimates, and the company revealed a new customer, which saw bullish reactions from analysts.

data.title: Pullback presents buying opportunities as market will resume higher – PNC’s Yung-Yu Ma
data.description:
PNC asset management chief investment strategist Yung-Yu Ma believes market pullbacks present buying opportunities despite growing concerns about weakening labor trends, as the market initially reacted optimistically to job numbers that solidified expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts, before shifting focus to worries about labor market momentum.

“We’re seeing weakening in the labor market. There’s no question now the trend is in place for a weakening labor market,” Ma said during an interview conducted by CNBC.

Despite these concerns, Ma expects the market to continue higher through the end of the year, with temporary volatility creating entry points for investors.

“We think September could be a choppy month. We think there could be a bit of a pullback as these concerns and uncertainties materialize over the coming months, but we actually think that’s an opportunity,” he said, and specifically advised investors to “lean into risk” given the Fed’s anticipated rate-cutting program.

Ma also addressed fears about AI-driven job displacement, suggesting technology advancements would primarily result in slower hiring rather than widespread layoffs. “Working with fewer people, but most of that should materialize into lower hires. We don’t expect mass layoffs to come from the productivity enhancements,” he said and emphasized that significant job cuts typically only occur during recessions.

However, he acknowledged that targeted reductions in the pharmaceutical (PPH), (PJP), (XPH) and energy (XLE) sectors would continue. He cited ConocoPhillips (COP) cutting a quarter of its workforce and similar efficiency-driven reductions across financial services companies.

“Is it going to happen at scale that it derails the overall economy? That’s the question,” he said.

Ma ultimately maintained an optimistic outlook, predicting that productivity gains would support economic stability rather than trigger a downturn. “We think those cuts are going to continue to boost profits.”

“They’re going to continue to drive productivity, but so much that actually tilts the economy toward a negative growth or recession and a downward momentum? We don’t think that is in the card.”

data.title: Google hit with nearly $3.5B fine in Europe over ad-tech
data.description:
Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) on Friday was hit with a fine of nearly €3B (almost $3.5B) by the European Commission for abusing market power in advertising technology -- a penalty that was delayed slightly amid trade threats from U.S. President Trump.

The fine was widely expected to come down on Monday, but an unusual late intervention by Maroš Šefcovic, EU trade commissioner, paused the decision amid tariff pressure from Trump against countries he said were discriminating against U.S. tech.

Competition Commissioner Teresa Ribera had pressed to go forward with the fine as planned.

The fine backs up the EC's existing contention that Google distorted competition in ad-tech in violation of European Union rules.

"It did so by favoring its own online display advertising technology services to the detriment of competing providers of advertising technology services, advertisers and online publishers," the EC said in a release.

"The Commission has ordered Google (i) to bring these self-preferencing practices to an end; and (ii) to implement measures to cease its inherent conflicts of interest along the adtech supply chain. Google has now 60 days to inform the Commission about how it intends to do so," the EC said.

Alphabet stock stayed higher on Friday in U.S. trading: (NASDAQ:GOOG) +0.8%, (NASDAQ:GOOGL) +0.8%.

data.title: Fastenal falls after August daily sales growth rate declined from July
data.description:
Fastenal (NASDAQ:FAST) -4.5% in Friday's trading after the distributor of industrial and construction supplies disclosed its daily sales growth rate in August declined from July.

The company reported daily sales jumped 11.8% Y/Y in August to $33.2M, but the result was below July's 12.8% rate of growth to $32.5M; heavy manufacturing daily sales rose 11.7% in August vs. 13.6% in July, other manufacturing daily sales rose 12.5% in August vs. 14.2% in July, non-residential construction daily sales rose 11.5% in August vs. 5% in July, and other end markets daily sales rose 10.3% in August vs. 11.2% in July.

"Sales decelerated following a strong July and likely reflect market-share gains and gradually improved pricing as volume remains challenged in a sluggish industrial economy," Bloomberg Intelligence analyst Christopher Ciolino wrote.

Earlier this week, Fastenal (NASDAQ:FAST) said it began operating in its newly-built 298K sq ft distribution center in near Salt Lake City, Utah.

data.title: Abivax jumps amid takeover speculation
data.description:
Abivax (NASDAQ:ABVX) shares rose 6.9% in Paris amid takeover speculation. Abivax ADRs gained 6%.

There's speculation that Abivax (NASDAQ:ABVX) has attracted takeover interest, according to traders, who cited a Betaville "uncooked" alert that was circulating on Friday. 

The potential suitor is a large American pharmaceutical company, people following the matter told Betaville. 

Abivax (ABVX) is scheduled to report its results on Monday. 

Abivax shares surged 510% in Paris on July 23 after the French biotech announced that its lead asset, obefazimod,  reached the primary goals in two Phase 3 trials for ulcerative colitis, an inflammatory bowel disorder.

data.title: Rate cuts don't stimulate growth, U.S. economy is ‘slowing slowly' – JPMorgan
data.description:
JPMorgan Asset Management chief global strategist David Kelly warned that the Federal Reserve's interest rate cuts will not stimulate growth for the U.S.'s economy, which is slowing down.

In an interview with CNBC, Kelly noted that recent economic data, including weaker job numbers, indicate a softening economy, saying “the economy is not in recession yet, but it is slowing slowly.”

Moreover, the chief strategist downplayed the potential effectiveness of Federal Reserve rate cuts in reviving economic growth.

“The whole history of the 21st century is rate cuts don’t stimulate growth,” he said, suggesting that lowering rates might actually harm retirees’ interest income while creating a wait-and-see mentality among potential borrowers.

Kelly identified uncertainty as the primary drag on economic performance, dubbing it “the biggest tax that the government levies.” He pointed to changing policies on tariffs, immigration, geopolitics, and the dollar (DXY) as factors causing businesses to hesitate on expansion and hiring decisions. “When everybody decides to wait and see, what you see is not good,” he warned.

On the topic of tariffs, Kelly expressed concern about their unpredictable implementation. He advocated for a stable tariff framework, stating that American businesses are “very adaptive” and “very good at growing if you tell them what the rules are.”

Kelly also emphasized that constant changes in trade policy make it difficult for manufacturers to make long-term investment decisions.

Despite current headwinds, he maintained that the U.S. economy should be capable of steady growth. “There’s no reason why this economy should be grinding to a halt,” he said, suggesting that with reduced policy uncertainty, the economy could achieve a steady growth rate of about 2% given America’s “most energetic enterprising people in the world.”

data.title: ABM Industries signals $35M annual savings through restructuring while advancing AI and share repurchases
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ABM Industries (ABM) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Scott Salmirs, President and CEO, introduced David Orr as the new CFO, highlighting "our cash flow performance in Q3" as a benefit from Orr's leadership. Salmirs reported "5% organic revenue growth, generated strong free cash flow and continued to win new business despite an uncertain macro environment." He noted over $150 million in free cash flow and a 15% year-over-year increase in bookings, totaling over $1.5 billion in new business during the first three quarters. Salmirs emphasized strategic pricing moves, stating these "did pressure margins and adjusted EPS," but led to multiyear contracts and extensions that support sustainable growth. The company launched a restructuring program expected to generate at least $35 million in annual run rate savings by year-end, with actions already underway.
  • Salmirs announced the purchase of more than 1 million shares during July and August and nearly 1.5 million shares year-to-date for $71.3 million. He added, "our Board increased our share repurchase authorization by $150 million after the quarter closed." Investments in AI were highlighted, including automation for RFP responses and HR support, with future plans to leverage AI for revenue streams and operational efficiency.
  • Salmirs reviewed segment trends: B&I saw a return to organic growth, M&D momentum was fueled by AI-driven technology investments, e-commerce, and reshoring, Aviation benefited from rising passenger demand and airport reinvestment, Education remained stable, and Technical Solutions' electrification business accounted for nearly 60% of segment revenue. He stated, "we expect our fourth quarter earnings and margin to improve meaningfully from the third quarter."
  • David Orr, Executive VP & CFO, reported "Revenue grew 6.2% year-over-year to $2.2 billion, driven by 5% organic revenue growth and a 1.2% contribution from our recent acquisitions." Orr detailed net income of $41.8 million or $0.67 per diluted share, and adjusted net income of $51.7 million or $0.82 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA was $125.8 million, with a margin of 5.9%. Orr noted the restructuring program is "expected to yield annualized savings of $35 million at a cost of approximately $10 million." Segment highlights included B&I revenue surpassing $1 billion, Aviation revenue at $291.8 million, M&D revenue at $408.9 million, Education revenue at $235.1 million, and Technical Solutions revenue at $249.5 million. Orr reported free cash flow of $150 million, ending liquidity at $691 million, and total debt of $1.6 billion.

Outlook

  • Management expects fourth quarter earnings and margin to improve meaningfully, driven by restructuring and strong ATS segment performance. The company now anticipates full year adjusted EPS and adjusted EBITDA margin to be toward the lower end of the prior ranges, specifically $3.65 to $3.80 for adjusted EPS and 6.3% to 6.5% for adjusted EBITDA margin. Interest expense for Q4 is projected at approximately $25 million. Orr stated, "our outlook does not include any future positive or negative prior year self-insurance adjustments."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q3 2025 was $2.2 billion, with 5% organic growth and 1.2% from acquisitions. Net income was $41.8 million, or $0.67 per diluted share. Adjusted net income was $51.7 million, or $0.82 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA reached $125.8 million, with a flat margin at 5.9% compared to the prior year. Free cash flow was $150 million, improving $86 million year-over-year and $135 million over Q2. Segment results: B&I revenue exceeded $1 billion, Aviation $291.8 million, M&D $408.9 million, Education $235.1 million, Technical Solutions $249.5 million. Year-to-date share repurchases totaled 1.5 million shares for $71.3 million, with a $150 million increase in share repurchase authorization post-quarter.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Luke McFadden, William Blair: Asked about M&D growth and whether it was due to lapping headwinds or underlying momentum. Salmirs responded, "it's just about attacking strong end markets and investments we've made in salespeople...this is more about our focus and our expertise."
  • McFadden asked about free cash flow in Q4. Orr explained, "free cash flow of $180 million to $220 million. We're at $42 million free cash flow year-to-date. So that would imply we need to do about $140 million in the fourth quarter to achieve the range."
  • Jasper Bibb, Truist Securities: Inquired about margin headwinds. Salmirs described a mix of defensive moves in B&I and opportunistic expansion in M&D, providing client negotiation examples that led to "acceptable" but lower margins in exchange for long-term contracts. He noted, "we're looking at discretionary costs, we're going back and relooking at labor efficiencies across our entire platform."
  • Bibb asked about collections and delinquencies. Orr stated, "our DSOs were down 7% sequentially Q2 to Q3."
  • Andrew J. Wittmann, Baird: Questioned the Q4 earnings ramp. Orr anticipated "roughly 100 basis points of improvement in margin," citing ATS segment performance and restructuring benefits.
  • Wittmann asked about timing of margin issues. Salmirs responded, "it was just bad timing...a bunch of clients concurrently come to us and talk about the fact that they're pressured."
  • Faiza Alwy, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the competitive environment. Salmirs responded, "there's not new entrants...there is nothing in our mind that's structural that's going on here."
  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Queried the magnitude of margin headwinds and Q4 catch-up. Orr said, "just the volume and aggregation of this quarter was something we have not historically seen...we anticipate roughly 20% of those benefits to come through in Q4 this year, with the balance to come through in fiscal '26."
  • Marc Riddick, Sidoti: Asked about contract visibility and retention. Salmirs explained, "our salespeople and our operators know how to provide savings and restructure the contracts...over the next 2 to 5 years, you see the margins come way back."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' sentiment was slightly negative, with repeated questions on margin pressure, the sustainability of growth, and the sequencing of contract renegotiations. Analysts pressed on the timing and magnitude of margin impacts and expressed skepticism about the competitive environment and episodic nature of client negotiations.
  • Management maintained a confident but defensive tone, especially when addressing margin headwinds and pricing negotiations. Salmirs repeatedly emphasized long-term strategic benefits, using phrases like "we're really enthusiastic" and highlighting proactive measures. In response to analyst pressure, management often provided detailed explanations and examples to reinforce confidence in recovery and strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the overall sentiment shifted from a more optimistic and growth-focused tone to a slightly more defensive and pragmatic stance, especially around margins and cost measures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from reaffirming full year ranges in Q2 to guiding towards the low end for both adjusted EPS and EBITDA margin in Q3, with higher interest expense noted as a key factor.
  • Strategic focus expanded from growth and market recovery to include significant cost control and restructuring, with $35 million in annualized savings targeted.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from expansion opportunities and cash flow normalization in Q2 to margin pressures, renegotiation timing, and competitive threats in Q3.
  • Key metrics such as organic revenue growth and free cash flow improved sequentially, but margin pressures became more pronounced due to pricing actions and client negotiations.
  • Management tone in Q2 was more optimistic, highlighting growth records and segment expansion, while Q3 featured a mix of confidence and defensive explanations about margin and cost management.
  • Analyst tone in Q2 was largely positive with curiosity on growth drivers, shifting to skepticism and concern over sustainability of margins and sequencing of renegotiations in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited slower recoveries in select commercial office markets, particularly West Coast, Midwest, and Mid-Atlantic regions.
  • Margin pressure due to strategic pricing decisions in response to client cost cutting and competitive threats was acknowledged.
  • Higher interest expense weighed on guidance and future profitability.
  • Management outlined mitigation strategies including labor efficiency measures, discretionary cost controls, and a restructuring program targeting $35 million in annual savings.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the episodic nature of contract renegotiations impacting margins and the sustainability of current growth and cash flow trends.

Final Takeaway

ABM Industries highlighted strong organic revenue growth, robust new business bookings, and improved free cash flow in Q3 2025, offset by margin pressures resulting from strategic pricing and client retention actions. The company launched a restructuring program expected to yield $35 million in annual savings and increased its share repurchase authorization to enhance capital flexibility. While management expressed confidence in long-term growth, they guided towards the low end of previous EPS and EBITDA margin ranges, reflecting higher interest expense and near-term cost headwinds. The company continues to invest in AI and operational efficiencies, positioning ABM for sustained value creation as market conditions evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Porsche EVs can now access Tesla superchargers
data.description:
To promote the adoption of its electric vehicles, Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) EV owners will have “soft launch” access to the Tesla (TSLA) supercharging network in North America beginning on September 9.

“We are continually working to make every aspect of Porsche ownership as convenient as possible for our customers – and this news represents a significant step for owners of our electrified cars,” said CEO of Porsche Cars North America Timo Resch.

Access will be possible through Porsche-supplied NACS DC adapters. At this soft launch stage, Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) customers will utilize the Tesla (TSLA) app to enable charging. Charging through the My Porsche app and plug-and-charge is expected to follow in the coming months. 2025 model Porsche Taycan and all existing Macan EV owners are eligible for a complimentary adapter. All other vehicle owners can purchase an adapter for $185. Beginning with 2026 model vehicles, NACS DC adapters will be provided at no additional cost.

By making EV charging more accessible to North American owners, Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) hopes to achieve faster adoption of their EV models which have received mixed demand. While the all-electric Macan has seen strong sales, the 1019-horsepower Taycan has been less well-received, given its steep sticker price ($100K to $200K), limited range compared to the Tesla Model S, and difficulty convincing Porsche enthusiasts to transition to an all-electric sportscar.

The development underpinned Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) shares in U.S. markets, up nearly 2% and reversing most of this week’s losses.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Bullish's German unit wins MiCA license for crypto services across EU
data.description:
Bullish's (NYSE:BLSH) German arm has secured Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) licensing from the German Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin), allowing the crypto exchange to offer its services anywhere in the European Union, it said on Friday.

"Europe is the second largest cryptoasset economy in the world and institutional demand for regulated, transparent trading venues is growing rapidly," said Chris Tyrer, president of Bullish Exchange. "We believe that our MiCAR approval allows us to continue to meet that demand head-on, by offering the trusted execution that institutions need to confidently engage with digital assets."

Bullish Europe was previously grandfathered through its crypto brokerage and custody licenses from BaFin.

The company will continue to be overseen by BaFin, it noted, as firms remain under the supervision of their home country regulator under MiCAR.

Meanwhile, Bullish (NYSE:BLSH) shares drifted down 2% in late morning trading, hovering around session lows.

data.title: ServiceTitan soars on Q2 results, higher guidance; analysts like new partnerships
data.description:
ServiceTitan (NASDAQ:TTAN) shares soared 10% during early market action on Friday as the software platform for field and residential service businesses reported better-than-expected second quarter results.

"ServiceTitan announced a strategic deal with Roto-Rooter (largest provider of plumbing, drain cleaning, and water cleanup services in North America) in conjunction with the Q2 earnings release, which is expected to go-live in early 2026," said Piper Sandler analysts led by Brent Bracelin in an investor note. "This partnership serves as yet another proof point of the breadth, enterprise readiness, and scalability of the platform."

Piper Sandler maintained its Overweight rating and increased its price target to $155 from $150.

KeyBanc also reiterated its Overweight rating but kept its $140 price target.

"Incorporating the quarterly outperformance, ServiceTitan raised its FY26 revenue guide by $22.5M to $935M-$940M (up from $910M-$920M), which now implies 21.5% growth (a 2pt increase), translating to an $8M net raise," said KeyBanc analysts Jason Celino in a note. "OM guidance also ticked up nicely, now at 8.0% (from 6.2%). Additionally, management noted that it now expects to overachieve its 25% incremental margin target for FY26."

Salesforce (CRM), which also offers field service software, was up 2% during Friday trading.
